body_txt: The tendency of fight scenes involving an Action Girl to also feature her doing impractical backflips, gratuitous somersaults , and gymnastic cartwheels to fight her opponents. This can overlap with Dance Battler , given that several real-life styles of dance employ motions and movements similar to kicks and acrobatic martial arts moves, and also because dancing is traditionally a feminine thing . It can also overlap with Waif-Fu , as she-fu practitioners are often shown inflicting physics-defying damage with even the most delicate-looking of their attacks, and Kick Chick , as kicks and anything that shows their legs in plenty are traditionally fanservicey. Finally, when the acrobatics are used in conjunction with the environment, it can cross into Combat Parkour . In live-action works, there's often some justification on the abilities of the actress in question; dance and gymnastics training is actually better suited to fight scenes than actual fight training - because movie fighting is after all choreography that usually has to be repeated multiple times while still looking good on camera. Although this trope lends itself well to Fanservice , the additional agility, dodging, and Spectacular Spinning is typically also encouraged by the Rule of Cool , especially with male characters. How the trope is handled as sensual or cool is really a spectrum that will very greatly between authors and filmmakers. This trope really began back when scantly clad female superheroes first started appearing in comic books, as artist wanted a way to make them look attractive while also looking cool in combat. This was most famously spearheaded by Sheena, Queen of the Jungle as an acrobatic Fur Bikini clad Jungle Princess , and other female heroines like Wonder Woman followed soon after. It wasn’t really until the trope moved east in the over the top " wuxia "-style action films from Hong Kong , that this trope evolved in the public eye to be about looking cool, especially when male characters like Spider-Man started embracing this trope back in the west. Even so, this day, the question of whether the author intended to make a fight scene unnecessarily awesome or just show off every part of the character’s body on camera is probably going to be raise upon publication. Usually it’s only the former with male characters, but neither sex is exclusive. It also has its roots on the strong reluctance, still active amongst modern-day writers, to feature an attractive young woman taking a full-on punch to the face (unless, of course, it's from a person of the same sex ), which forces them to gift her with implausible dodging abilities that will allow her not to be even touched. There is also some Truth in Television to this trope, as women are naturally somewhat more agile than men, and much lighter. With reduced size and weight and especially if she is fighting a much stronger opponent a style that focuses on fudging blows and outpacing an opponent makes some sense. For the extreme of fanservice over realism, see Cat Fight . Do not confuse with Shaq Fu , the indie Beat 'em Up Sifu (though they do have an example) or the character Shifu from Kung Fu Panda , whose name is (mis)pronounced the same way but spelled differently, and means "master" in Chinese (but only if you pronounce it correctly).
